text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Renata Okonkwo
DOB: on file
Attending: Hematology/Oncology
Visit type: Follow-up, Adjuvant Therapy Monitoring

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Return visit for oncology follow-up. Patient completing adjuvant Xeloda (cycle 8 of 8) and continuing Keytruda maintenance. Presenting for toxicity assessment, laboratory review, and planning of remaining therapy and transition to surveillance.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo is a 39-to-40-year-old Nigerian-American woman with a confirmed BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant (c.5074del, p.Cys1692ValfsTer17, heterozygous) who was originally diagnosed with Stage IIB (T2 N1 M0) Grade 3 triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast at age 39. Her tumor was ER-negative, PR-negative, HER2 1+ by IHC (negative per ASCO/CAP guidelines), with a Ki-67 proliferation index of 85% and a basal-like immunophenotype (CK5/6+, EGFR 2+). NGS demonstrated a TP53 p.R248W gain-of-function mutation, PTEN homozygous deletion, RB1 nonsense mutation, somatic BRCA1 frameshift (concordant with germline finding), MYC high-level amplification, and PD-L1 CPS of 18 by 22C3 assay. She was enrolled on a KEYNOTE-522--type regimen and received neoadjuvant pembro 200 mg IV every 3 weeks in combination with dose-dense Adriamycin (60 mg/m2) and cyclo (600 mg/m2) every 2 weeks with pegfilgrastim support for 4 cycles, followed by weekly pacli 80 mg/m2 for 12 weeks (dose-reduced to 64 mg/m2 after Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy developed around weeks 6-9) plus pembro every 3 weeks for 4 additional doses. Neoadjuvant course was complicated by immune-related thyroiditis (CPS Grade 2 thyrotoxic phase followed by permanent hypothyroidism, now managed on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily), with pembro held for one cycle during the thyrotoxic phase. She also developed Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy and Grade 2 myalgia during the Taxol phase, necessitating the 20% dose reduction.

Post-neoadjuvant MRI demonstrated significant but incomplete response (residual 1.4 x 1.1 cm mass, down from 3.4 cm baseline). She underwent bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy with left axillary lymph node dissection and right prophylactic mastectomy with bilateral tissue expander placement. Final pathology showed ypT1b ypN1 M0 with residual invasive carcinoma and RCB Class II (non-pCR). Two of fourteen left axillary nodes harbored metastatic deposits (largest 4 mm); the sentinel lymph node contained isolated tumor cells (ypN0(i+) classification for the SLN, but ypN1 overall given the positive ALND nodes). Receptor status on residual tumor was unchanged (ER-/PR-/HER2 1+).

Given non-pCR status and CREATE-X data supporting adjuvant cape in TNBC, she was started on adjuvant Xeloda 1710 mg BID (1000 mg/m2 BID, BSA 1.71 m2) days 1-14 of each 21-day cycle, combined with pembro 200 mg IV every 21 days to complete 1 year of total pembro exposure per KEYNOTE-522. cape was dose-reduced by 25% to approximately 1250 mg BID starting with cycle 4 following Grade 2-3 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome) with painful blistering on both feet. Since the dose reduction, HFS has downgraded to Grade 1 and has been manageable with urea-based emollients. She also experienced Grade 1 diarrhea (managed PRN loperamide) and a transient Grade 1 ALT elevation (peak 52 U/L, resolved by cycle 5). No colitis, pneumonitis, or other new immune-related adverse events during the adjuvant phase to date.

Today she presents having completed cycle 8 (the final planned cycle) of cape. She is currently receiving pembro maintenance and has approximately 2 doses remaining to complete 1 year of total pembro therapy. She reports feeling generally fatigued but is coping. She is working part-time as a biology teacher and notes significant psychosocial stress related to her diagnosis at a young age, her BRCA1 carrier status, and concerns about her children's future risk.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: Persistent Grade 1 fatigue, slowly improving. No fever, chills, or unintentional weight loss.
- HEENT: No headache. No visual changes.
- Respiratory: No new dyspnea, cough, or chest pain. She notes occasional mild exertional fatigue but no resting dyspnea and no oxygen requirement. Prior Keytruda-related thyroiditis, no pulmonary symptoms currently.
- Cardiovascular: No palpitations at this time (prior palpitations during thyrotoxic phase resolved). No peripheral edema.
- GI: Mild residual loose stools, 1-2 episodes above baseline per day, manageable without loperamide currently. No nausea. No vomiting. Appetite decreased but stable. No abdominal pain.
- Dermatologic: Grade 1 bilateral palmar and plantar erythema, non-blistering, non-limiting. Significant improvement from peak Grade 2-3 HFS. Mild skin dryness. Ongoing Grade 2 alopecia (expected with prior anthracycline/taxane; hair regrowth slowly underway).
- Neurologic: Persistent bilateral fingertip tingling and mild toe numbness, consistent with Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy. She rates it 2/10 on a numeric scale and notes gradual improvement compared to peak during pacli. No motor weakness. No fall risk.
- Musculoskeletal: No significant arthralgias or myalgias currently (resolved from Taxol phase). No bone pain.
- Endocrine: Tolerating levothyroxine. Mild cold intolerance, but largely asymptomatic. No palpitations.
- Genitourinary: No dysuria. Last menstrual period timing uncertain given treatment-related cycle disruption; patient has not had a definitive period in several months (treatment-related amenorrhea, though she remains technically premenopausal per pre-surgical labs). Notes some vaginal dryness.
- Psychiatric/Psychosocial: Reports ongoing anxiety related to cancer recurrence fears and BRCA1 carrier status, particularly regarding implications for her two children. Engaged with oncology social work. Working with a therapist. Sleep mildly disrupted.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vitals: BP 112/72 mmHg, HR 74 bpm (regular), RR 16, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 66 kg (down approximately 2 kg from initiation of cape; overall 4 kg below her pre-treatment weight).
ECOG Performance Status: 1 (restricted in strenuous activity, ambulatory, able to carry out work of a light or sedentary nature).

General: Alert, oriented, well-appearing. Mild temporal hair thinning. In no acute distress.
HEENT: PERRL, EOMI. No icterus. Oropharynx moist; trace mucositis on right buccal mucosa, Grade 1, non-limiting. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular regions.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Good air movement.
Breast/Chest wall: Bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy sites well-healed with intact incisions. Bilateral tissue expanders in place, symmetric, no overlying erythema, warmth, or fluctuance. No palpable masses in the chest wall. No axillary, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular lymphadenopathy appreciated bilaterally. Left axillary surgical changes noted.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. Normal bowel sounds. No palpable masses.
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or significant edema. Bilateral feet: mild erythema of plantar surfaces, diffuse, non-blistering, consistent with resolving HFS Grade 1. Bilateral palmar erythema, patchy, mild. Skin not cracked. No open lesions.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Mild decreased light touch sensation in bilateral fingertips and plantar surfaces of feet. Gait normal and steady. Romberg negative.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC (most recent, this cycle): WBC 5.8 x10^9/L; ANC 3,400/uL; Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, unchanged from prior); Platelets 218 x10^9/L.
CMP: Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 3.9 mEq/L, Creatinine 0.78 mg/dL (eGFR >60), Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, ALT 31 U/L (normal, resolved from prior cycle 4-5 elevation), AST 28 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 72 U/L.
TSH: 2.8 mIU/L (within normal range on levothyroxine 75 mcg). Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (within normal limits).
Blood glucose: 92 mg/dL (fasting).
No cortisol or ACTH stimulation testing indicated at this time (no clinical concern for adrenal insufficiency).
PD-L1 re-testing: Not performed on residual/recurrent specimen (no current recurrence).

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Surveillance CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast (performed during this adjuvant therapy period, approximately 6 months after surgery): Bilateral tissue expanders in place without evidence of locoregional recurrence at the chest wall or axilla. No pulmonary nodules or pleural effusions. No hepatic lesions. No mediastinal, hilar, axillary, or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Osseous structures intact; no lytic or blastic lesions. No adnexal pathology. Overall impression: No evidence of distant metastatic disease or locoregional recurrence. This is reassuring given her non-pCR status and RCB Class II residual disease burden.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. TRIPLE-NEGATIVE BREAST CANCER, STAGE IIB (ypT1b ypN1 M0 POST-NEOADJUVANT, RCB CLASS II, BRCA1 GERMLINE CARRIER) -- NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE
Ms. Okonkwo has now completed 8 full cycles of adjuvant Xeloda (CREATE-X--based regimen, at dose-reduced level of approximately 1250 mg BID given prior Grade 2-3 HFS). She has responded to the dose reduction with manageable Grade 1 residual toxicity. Per our discussion today, Xeloda is now COMPLETE. She continues pembro 200 mg IV every 21 days; she has approximately 2 doses remaining to fulfill the planned 1-year total pembro duration as specified per KEYNOTE-522. pembro will be administered as scheduled. She will continue to be monitored closely for immune-related adverse events with each Keytruda dose. Surveillance imaging reviewed as above -- no evidence of recurrence or distant metastasis at this time, which is very encouraging.

I reviewed with her the residual risks given non-pCR and RCB Class II status in the setting of TNBC: the highest-risk period for recurrence in TNBC is the first 2-3 years, with visceral sites (lung, liver, CNS) most common. We discussed the importance of prompt reporting of new neurologic symptoms, persistent cough, dyspnea, bone pain, or other new symptoms.

Lynparza (Lynparza) was again discussed as an adjuvant option given BRCA1 carrier status and OlympiA trial data demonstrating disease-free survival benefit in high-risk HER2-negative early breast cancer with germline BRCA mutations. Patient and I reviewed: she completed neoadjuvant therapy and had non-pCR, she is BRCA1 germline positive, and she technically meets eligibility criteria for adjuvant Lynparza per OlympiA. However, given that she has been on an extended course of chemotherapy (including anthracycline, taxane, and Xeloda) as well as immunotherapy, and given her current toxicity burden (fatigue, neuropathy, HFS recovery), we have elected -- consistent with her preference and shared decision-making -- to complete the pembro course first and reassess before adding Lynparza. This is a reasonable approach. We will re-discuss at the next visit after she completes pembro whether she wishes to pursue adjuvant Lynparza (300 mg PO BID x 1 year) given the additional DFS benefit, weighing her fatigue, neuropathy, and quality of life concerns. The patient is aware of this option and will inform her decision over the next 4-6 weeks.

2. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY -- GRADE 1, IMPROVING
Bilateral fingertip and plantar numbness consistent with chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN), predominantly from prior Taxol exposure. Now Grade 1, improved from Grade 2 peak during weekly Taxol. No motor involvement. Reassuring trajectory. I reassured Ms. Okonkwo that ongoing improvement over 6-12 months is expected for many patients, though some residual neuropathy may persist. No pharmacologic intervention for neuropathy currently indicated (does not meet threshold for duloxetine by ASCO guidelines given Grade 1 severity). She is encouraged to continue gentle hand and foot exercises, avoid tight footwear, and continue urea-based moisturizers for the skin. Will monitor at next visit.

3. IMMUNE-MEDIATED HYPOTHYROIDISM -- STABLE ON LEVOTHYROXINE
pembro-induced immune thyroiditis with permanent hypothyroidism, well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH 2.8 mIU/L at this visit. Symptoms (mild cold intolerance) are minimal. She should continue levothyroxine indefinitely. TSH will be rechecked in approximately 3 months or sooner if symptoms change. Endocrinology has been following; she will maintain that relationship for ongoing thyroid management. No dose adjustment required at this time.

4. HAND-FOOT SYNDROME -- GRADE 1, RESOLVING AFTER Xeloda COMPLETION
HFS peaked at Grade 2-3 during cycles 2-3 of Xeloda, with painful plantar blistering, and improved to Grade 1 on the reduced dose. Now with Xeloda complete, I expect gradual further resolution over the next 2-4 weeks. She should continue consistent application of urea-based or petroleum-based emollients to palms and soles. No further Xeloda dose adjustments are relevant given completion of the planned 8-cycle course.

5. PSYCHOSOCIAL DISTRESS AND BRCA1 CARRIER STATUS
Ms. Okonkwo continues to report significant anxiety and emotional burden related to her diagnosis at a young age, BRCA1 status, and concerns about her two children. She is engaged with oncology social work and is working with an individual therapist, which I commend. We discussed that her BRCA1 c.5074del pathogenic variant is hereditary; her children have a 50% chance of having inherited it, and offer of genetic testing at age 18-25 (or earlier if planning families) was reiterated. She is encouraged to involve her siblings and parents in genetic counseling discussions as well, as first-degree relatives should be offered cascade testing.

Regarding risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO): she underwent laparoscopic RRSO (fallopian tubes and ovaries removed, uterus preserved per her preference) at age 40, with pathology demonstrating no evidence of malignancy and no STIC lesions -- an excellent result. This is expected to meaningfully reduce her lifetime ovarian cancer risk (from approximately 44-46% to <2%). She is now in surgical menopause. We discussed non-hormonal options for menopause symptoms (ongoing use of venlafaxine 75 mg for vasomotor symptoms, topical vaginal moisturizers). She remains ineligible for systemic hormone therapy given her breast cancer history.

Fertility: She had previously banked embryos prior to initiating chemotherapy; this remains available to her in the future if desired. Given surgical menopause (RRSO), natural conception is no longer possible.

6. SURVEILLANCE PLAN -- TRANSITION TO POST-TREATMENT SURVEILLANCE
Given that Xeloda is now complete and Keytruda will be completed within the next 6 weeks (2 remaining doses), we discussed transitioning to the survivorship/surveillance phase:
- Oncology follow-up: every 3-4 months for the first 2 years post-completion of therapy, then every 6 months through year 5, then annually. Given non-pCR status and BRCA1 mutation, closer follow-up is warranted in the near term.
- Annual chest wall/skin MRI of bilateral reconstructed breasts: standard for patients who have undergone mastectomy with BRCA1 mutation; this will be scheduled at 12 months post-surgery.
- CT surveillance: will continue per standard TNBC high-risk surveillance at 12-month intervals (or sooner if symptoms arise), particularly given non-pCR and RCB II.
- Tumor markers (CA 27.29, CA 15-3): not recommended per ASCO guidelines for routine surveillance in TNBC.
- Bone health: Given surgical menopause at age 40, DEXA scan recommended within 1-2 years to establish baseline bone mineral density; bisphosphonate therapy may be considered depending on result.
- High-risk program referral: Reiterated referral to our institutional hereditary breast cancer high-risk program for BRCA1-specific ongoing management, including right chest wall/skin surveillance.
- Plastic surgery: Tissue expander-to-implant exchange (stage 2 reconstruction) planned with plastic surgery once all adjuvant systemic therapy is completed. Scheduled for approximately 6-8 weeks after last Keytruda dose.

7. MEDICATIONS (CURRENT)
- Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (immune-mediated hypothyroidism)
- Venlafaxine 75 mg PO daily (hot flashes/surgical menopause, initiated post-RRSO)
- Urea 40% cream to hands and feet BID-TID (HFS management)
- Loperamide 2 mg PO PRN loose stools
- Ondansetron 8 mg PO PRN nausea (as-needed, rarely used currently)
- Calcium carbonate 600 mg + Vitamin D3 800 IU BID (bone health, initiated post-RRSO)
- cape 1250 mg PO BID days 1-14 (COMPLETED as of today, cycle 8 of 8)
- Keytruda 200 mg IV every 21 days (2 doses remaining; next dose as scheduled)

Return visit: In 3 weeks for next (penultimate) Keytruda dose, labs, and clinical assessment. Following that visit, one final Keytruda dose will complete the 1-year planned course. We will then schedule a comprehensive end-of-treatment survivorship visit at approximately 4-6 weeks post-completion. Patient is counseled to call immediately for: new dyspnea, cough, or hypoxia (pneumonitis); skin rash; diarrhea more than 4 stools above baseline (colitis); new neurologic symptoms; bone pain; or any other concerning new symptoms. Patient verbalized understanding and agreement with the plan. Questions addressed. She was accompanied by her sister today, who also had questions about cascade genetic testing -- I provided appropriate referral information.

Time spent: 45 minutes total (25 minutes face-to-face, 20 minutes chart review and care coordination).
